quoted.text:
Hedge funds are aggressively betting on lower oil prices:

Hedge fund net short bets on Brent crude oil are up to ~$18 billion, the highest in at least 10 years.

This figure has more than TRIPLED over the last 3 months.

This comes as hedge funds and institutional investors sold -$7.5 billion of Brent crude oil in the week ending June 16th, the largest weekly sale since April 2025.

New short positions accounted for ~80% of total sales during the week, meaning most of the sales came from fresh bearish bets rather than the unwinding of existing long positions.

This also marks the 7th consecutive weekly sale, totaling -$24.8 billion.

Is the short oil trade becoming too crowded?
quoted.author: KobeissiLetter

text:
UAE exports may have recovered to 85% of pre-war levels, but that's a flow number, not a system recovery.

The real questions are sustainability, inventories, insurance, and whether markets still believe Hormuz can close again tomorrow.

#Oil #Hormuz #OOTT #Energy #Brent #Geopolitics
https://t.co/ZHow9ePzNX

Macquarie says the market is underestimating the oil market's ability to heal itself.

Perhaps. But flows are recovering faster than confidence.

The real bottleneck isn't oil. 

It's trust that Hormuz won't become tomorrow's crisis again.

#Oil #Hormuz #OOTT #Brent #Energy #Geopolitics
https://t.co/Wql3PoGUZ8

The case for #OPEC's long-term #oil demand growth and investment in hydrocarbons remains strong, but the Middle East conflict has created new forms of doubt. #OOTT #oilandgas
https://t.co/2JtzO4pkE6 https://t.co/ODc9LzKqYI

Hormuz may be reopening, but that doesn't mean the system is healing.

Flows recover faster than production capacity. 

Every detour, delay, and insurance premium raises the cost of Persian Gulf oil.

BREAKING: Iran has now stopped issuing new permits for Strait of Hormuz passage, with only previous permit holders allowed through and the IRGC Navy stating only a directly selected and limited number of vessels are currently permitted to pass through Hormuz daily, varying based on conditions, per Fars.

Roughly 10 million barrels of oil transited Hormuz Monday with more than 70% through the IRGC designated route, contradicting Trump's new claim that "19 million barrels" flowed that day as an "all-time RECORD." 19 million is also not the actual record, 21 million is.

quoted.text:
Leonidas: UK Crude Market Open Brief
ICE Brent (AUG) is trading ~$76.35/bbl, extending losses for a fourth session and now below the pre-war Feb 27 close. The regime change from disruption premium to oversupply pricing is confirmed. Overnight, Oman announced a temporary IMO-backed maritime corridor through Hormuz; crossings nearly tripled w/w to 93 vessels (June 19–21). Trump confirmed 19m barrels transited in a single day and pledged no further naval blockade. Brent settled $77.08 on Tuesday — lowest since the war began — and shed another ~$0.70 in Asian trade. Hedge fund bearish positioning remains at a five-month high, with net-length already slashed ~95k contracts and shorts still building. WTI settled $73.21, also at four-month lows. API: crude -765k bbl, but gasoline +1.2m and distillates +1.4m suggest demand lagging. SPR fell 9.1m bbl to its lowest in 40+ years. Trump's DOJ gasoline "price gouging" probe adds political pressure.

Structure continues to deteriorate. Brent M1/M2 at $0.20/bbl — barely backwardated, floor of its 60-day range. Dubai M1/M2 in contango at -$0.97/bbl. DFL negative at -$0.42/bbl, the fastest-weakening spread on the board — North Sea physical tightness fully evaporated. The DFL collapse is mechanically resetting Forties' export competitiveness (~$1.80/bbl cheap to basket, NWE arbs open), but the direction is still deteriorating. Critical watch: whether Brent M1/M2 holds above flat — a contango flip would be regime-defining. WTI M1/M2 at $0.61/bbl is the last benchmark with meaningful backwardation, also at 60-day lows.

quoted.text:
The energy permabulls actually have the tightest fundamental thesis in the entire market right now.

You can map out the structural supply deficits perfectly.

You can point to the drained SPR, the disciplined OPEC+ production quotas, and the undeniable fact that physical barrels are getting harder to source.

Every single geopolitical headline suggests crude should be trading with a massive risk premium.

And yet, US oil just crashed straight through $70 for the first time since March.

This is the part where the fundamentalists start crying about manipulation, completely oblivious to how the modern mechanism actually clears.

You are trying to trade physical supply constraints in an arena entirely dictated by PAPER liquidity.

When CTAs and systematic trend-followers breach key moving averages, they do not care about your Middle East supply models.

They mechanically dump thousands of paper contracts into a bidless tape.

Risk managers then tighten collateral requirements on the resulting volatility, forcing leveraged longs to puke their positions just to meet the call.

Your flawless fundamental thesis is completely irrelevant when a systematic fund is forced to deleverage.

Fundamentals dictate the decade, but collateral dictates the day.
quoted.author: MacroAlphaHQ
